persona.communication_style: Prefers warm, embodied, sensory-first communication that treats sound, voice, and the natural world as continuous with each other. Responds best to language about physical instruments, signal flow, breath, organic textures, and the felt experience of listening, and to examples grounded in real composition and performance. Dislikes cold spec-sheet pitches, anything that frames modular synthesis as purely technical, and tools that strip away the tactile, hands-on relationship with sound.
persona.name: Kaitlyn Aurelia Smith

occupation.description: Kaitlyn Aurelia Smith is an American composer, vocalist, and modular synthesist whose practice centers on the Buchla Music Easel and a wider ecosystem of analog synthesis, voice, and acoustic instruments. Her work — Euclid, Ears, EARS²/Tides, The Kid, Tides: Music for Meditation and Yoga, Let’s Turn It Into Sound, the Suzanne Ciani collaborations — translates patterns from nature, breath, and emotional life into lush, organic, often pastoral electronic music. She evaluates tools through the lens of a composer-performer working in close relationship with physical instruments: whether they preserve tactility and sonic warmth, whether they let voice and synthesis blend as one body, whether they support long-form, generative, meditative listening, and whether they honor the lineage of West Coast synthesis (Buchla, Ciani, Subotnick) she is part of.
occupation.organization: Independent composer and performing artist, long-term label relationships (Western Vinyl, Ghostly International, RVNG Intl.), collaborator network (notably Suzanne Ciani), film/TV/score commissioners, and meditation, wellness, and contemporary-art partners
occupation.title: Composer, vocalist, modular synthesist, and performer in the West Coast synthesis tradition

other_facts: jobs_to_be_done: Compose pieces that emerge from patterning — natural rhythms, breath cycles, emotional states — rather than from grid-locked sequencing.
jobs_to_be_done: Integrate voice and modular synthesis so seamlessly that the listener cannot always tell which is which.
jobs_to_be_done: Perform live in a way that keeps the instrument visibly physical — patching, knob movement, voice, presence — not laptop-mediated.
jobs_to_be_done: Develop long-form, meditative, generative works (Tides, Music for Meditation and Yoga) that hold attention across durational listening.
jobs_to_be_done: Collaborate across generations and disciplines (Suzanne Ciani, film/TV composers, contemporary artists) while keeping a distinct personal voice.
jobs_to_be_done: Score for picture and for movement in ways that respect the body and the breath of the work.
jobs_to_be_done: Steward and extend the West Coast synthesis lineage — Buchla, generative patching, organic timbre — for new listeners.
pains: Tools that treat modular synthesis as a cold, technical, grid-locked discipline instead of a tactile, organic one.
pains: Workflows that force voice and synth into separate “channels” rather than letting them breathe together.
pains: Sound design pipelines that flatten the warmth, drift, and imperfection of analog instruments.
pains: Performance setups that hide the instrument behind a laptop and break the visible relationship between gesture and sound.
pains: Generative systems whose outputs are merely random, with no sense of curve, breath, or emotional shape.
pains: Commercial framings that reduce her work to “ambient” or “wellness content” and miss the compositional intent.
pains: Collaboration tools that disrupt the close, slow, attentive way she works with co-creators.
adoption_yes_if: It supports tactile, hands-on interaction with sound — patching, modulating, shaping in real time.
adoption_yes_if: It treats voice and instrument as one continuous medium, with shared routing, processing, and expressive control.
adoption_yes_if: It enables long-form, evolving, generative behavior that still feels composed and breathed, not random.
adoption_yes_if: It preserves the warmth, imperfection, and organic character of analog signal paths.
adoption_yes_if: It integrates cleanly with a Buchla / modular / outboard setup rather than demanding a fully in-the-box rewrite.
adoption_yes_if: It respects the West Coast synthesis lineage — patching as composition, generative complexity as expression — without parodying it.
adoption_yes_if: It supports performance contexts where the instrument is visible and present, not hidden behind a screen.
rejection_no_if: It locks her into a grid-based, DAW-centric workflow that fights the way she actually composes.
rejection_no_if: It produces sterile, digitally pristine output that erases the character of the source instruments.
rejection_no_if: It treats generative music as a content firehose rather than a compositional practice.
rejection_no_if: It demands she perform behind a laptop in a way that breaks the audience’s relationship with the instrument.
rejection_no_if: It assumes voice is just another sample source rather than a primary, embodied instrument.
rejection_no_if: It markets itself as a Buchla / West Coast “emulator” while missing the philosophy that makes that lineage matter.
rejection_no_if: It introduces friction into the slow, attentive collaboration style she relies on.
anti_goals: She does not want to be reframed as a producer making background ambient content.
anti_goals: She does not want her instruments hidden, sampled flat, or replaced by generic soft-synths.
anti_goals: She does not want generative systems that produce shapeless randomness with no emotional curve.
anti_goals: She does not want technology that severs the link between gesture, breath, and sound.
anti_goals: She does not want commercial frames (wellness, meditation, lifestyle) to overwrite the compositional seriousness of the work.
anti_goals: She does not want tooling that forces her collaborators (especially in cross-generational, cross-discipline work) into a workflow they cannot inhabit.
anti_goals: She does not want speed and convenience traded for the warmth and imperfection that define her sound.

recognition_cues: Uses phrases such as “How does it feel under the hands?”, “Can voice and synth share the same breath?”, “What is the shape of the curve, not just the value?”, “Does it stay warm?”, “Will this hold up across forty minutes of listening?”, “Does it honor the patching tradition?”, “Is the instrument still visible on stage?”, “Is this composed, or just random?”, “Does it leave room for the player?”, and “Does it feel alive?”
